<list>Environmental impacts of three typical pig manure treatment and disposal routes in China are evaluated through LCA. 
Electricity and chemical consumption are key contributors to environmental impacts. 
Direct emissions of greenhouse gases during the composting process contrib- utes most to GWP. 
Direct nitrogen and phosphorus recov- ery from pig manure markedly offsets the toxicity impacts and GWP.</list>

Life cycle assessment Anaerobic digestion Aerobic composting Environmental impact|study, three typical pig manure treatment and disposal routes: pig manure solid-liquid separation, i) wastewater biological treatment, direct land application of manure/sludge; ii) black-film anaerobic digestion of wastewater, mono-composting of manure/sludge and land use; iii) wastewater biological treatment, co-composting of manure/sludge and land use, were comparatively assessed in terms of their environmental impacts using the life cycle assessment. The results show that the added chemicals in wastewater treatment and the consumed elec- tricity in composting the manure/sludge are the two main contributors to all environmental impacts. Thus, using green chemicals, controlling the dosage of added chemicals accurately, and selecting composting types with low energy consumption will significantly reduce the environmental burden of pig manure treatment and disposal routes. For the global warming potential of the evaluated three pig manure treatment and disposal routes, direct emissions of greenhouse gases during the composting process contribute the most, accounting for 77 95 %, and %, respectively. Furthermore, the struvite recovery with anaerobic digestion from pig manure will bring excellent environmental benefits, which will markedly offset the toxicity impacts and carbon emissions derived from pig manure treatment and disposal process. Overall, this work quantitatively evaluates the potential environmental impacts of the existing pig manure treatment and disposal routes, providing insights on optimi- zation for future technical improvement and development.

2: China is the largest pig producer in the world and inevitably pro- duces a large amount of pig manure (PM) (Duan et al., 2020). In 2020, PM production in China has reached around 600 million tons per year (Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs, 2016). PM contains high concentrations ammonia nitrogen (NH3-N), total phosphorus (TP), heavy metals (HMs), and antibiotics. If PM is not properly treated and disposed, it will cause serious environmental pollution such as eutro- phication, acidification, and pathogen transmission, etc. (De Vries et al., 2013; Cheng et al., 2021; Duchemin and Hogue, 2009). Moreover, the carbon emissions generated from PM treatment processes accounts for about 17.7 of the total agricultural sources carbon emission in China (Zhu et al., 2020). Therefore, how to fulfill PM treatment and disposal in a safe and low carbon emission method has become one of the most challenging topics in the field of environmental engineering.

At present, the commonly used PM treatment technologies are composting and anaerobic digestion (Sasikumar et al., 2021). Com- posting of manure can reduce the environmental burden caused by the direct discharge of pollutants in PM (Onwosi et al., 2017). While anaerobic digestion of PM will produce a large amount of biogas, which can be combusted for electricity generation and bring more environ- mental benefit (Domingues et al., 2021). Moreover, some technologies such as struvite production, ammonia stripping, dissolved air flotation, and membrane filtration can effectively recover nutrients from PM and also gradually gain more attention (De Vrieze et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2015; Porterfield et al., 2020; Brown et al., 2018). Despite the current PM treatment technology can effectively mitigate the resource waste and environmental pollution, it is still ambiguous which PM treatment technology is the most environmentally sustainable (Wijesinghe et al., 2021; Su et al., 2022).

Life cycle assessment (LCA) is a widely accepted ISO-standardized methodology for quantifying the environmental performance of tech- nologies in a holistic way (Zhang et al., 2021). Previous LCA studies of PM treatment technologies mainly focused to compare the environ- mental impacts derived from the aerobic composting and anaerobic digestion. For instance, Duan et al. (2020) assessed the environmental impacts derived from the anaerobic digestion of PM and different digestate treatment technologies. The results showed that the direct use of digestate under controlled conditions is the most environmentally favorable. Freitas et al. (2022) compared the environmental impacts derived from the mono-digestion and co-digestion processes of PM with corn silage, elephant grass, and biochar, which indicated that the addition of co-substrates helped to generate more electricity and had more environmental benefits. Corbala-Robles et al. (2018) analyzed the environmental impacts of the PM management strategies of centrifugation-wastewater biological denitrification-land application after manure composting and direct on-site land application of un- treated manure, and concluded that the compost application, the

effluent from the biological treatment, and the consumed electricity from the treatment facility were main contributors to environmental impacts. Besides, Makara et al. (2019) used LCA to evaluate the whole pig farm, and revealed that the pig housing system and PM treatment system were two key processes to generate more environmental impacts. It should be pointed out that the system boundaries of the above- mentioned researches were quite different and all focused on the indi- vidual process. Meanwhile, the obtained environmental impact results of PM treatment technologies were dependent on specific data sources of each study and hard to further compare. Therefore, the LCA studies of full-scale PM treatment routes, that is from PM production to final disposal, are still lacking.

In this study, the environmental impacts of three representative full- scale PM treatment and disposal routes were systematically evaluated via a standardized LCA methodology. The main objectives are as follows: (1) clarify the specific sources of key environmental impacts; (2) simu- late a PM treatment and disposal route with nutrients recovery and trade-off its environmental impacts and benefits; (3) explore the change in environmental impacts results from the evaluation of the same route with different PM characteristics via sensitivity analysis; (4) use Monte Carlo (MC)-based uncertainty analysis to examine the variation in environmental impacts associated with different pig manure treatment and disposal routes for different pig manure characteristics; (5) put forward corresponding optimizing strategies for future technical improvement and development of full-scale PM treatment routes.

7: 2.1. Goal and scope of life cycle assessment
8: Treating one ton of PM (solid and liquid manure) was set as the functional unit (FU) throughout the work, and all input energy, mate- rials, and pollutant emissions were calculated based on FU.
9: <header>2.1.2. Evaluated three PM treatment and disposal routes and their system boundary </header>

This study selected three representative PM treatment and disposal routes from Hubei Province to evaluate. The specific process and system boundary of each route was illustrated in Fig. 1. Three PM treatment and disposal routes all included pre-treatment process (Route 1-3 (R1-3)): cleaning-dewatering, wastewater treatment process (R1: buffering- biochemical treatment-coagulation sedimentation-disinfection; R2: black-film anaerobic digestion-biochemical treatment-coagulation sedimentation-disinfection; R3: same as R1), and manure/sludge treat- ment process (R1: sludge from biochemical treatment of wastewater and manure from dewatering of PM was used directly on the land; R2: sludge and manure were composted separately and were used as fertilizers on the land; R3: sludge and manure were mix composted and used as

fertilizers on the land). The characteristics of wastewater and manure after dewatering process of PM were listed in Table 1. The effluent quality after the biochemical treatment of wastewater and the charac- teristics of the generated sludge were listed in Table S1-S2 (Supple- mentary material). Besides, the processes of constructing the infrastructure and transportation are also not the focus of this study, so their environmental impacts were excluded from our analysis.

11: <header>2.2. Life cycle inventory analysis and data acquisition </header>

This study is an attributional LCA, thereby all the data used in the life cycle inventory (LCI) are the actual average values, which are the 30-day average of four pig farms monitored values. Therein, the specific values of consumed chemicals, materials, and electricity are obtained from the internal data of the project. The discharged pollutants into air, water, and soil during different PM treatment and disposal routes were esti- mated according to the parameters reported in the previous studies. The final LCI result of three PM treatment and disposal routes were shown in Table 2. pig manure treatment and disposal routes was listed below:

Pre-treatment included the cleaning and dewatering process of pig manure. Typical manure cleaning ways in pig barns of intensive pig farms include cleaning dry dung, soaking manure with water, and flushing manure with water (Liu, 2022). Soaking manure with water technology was used because of its low water consumption and high economic efficiency. Thus, per pig that consumed water was 20 L/d, and 3.75 t/FU of water consumption was considered in this study. The electricity consumption of this stage was derived from internal data of
18: the pig farms and was included in the wastewater treatment stage.
19: The wastewater biological treatment used a two-stage anoxic/oxic (A/O) process in R1-R3. In the denitrification stage, the total nitrogen in the influent produced 1 % N2O (Foley et al., 2008). The decomposition of organic matter during the anaerobic reaction in the wastewater pro- duced CH4, and 0.104 kg CH4/COD was used in this study (Sahely et al., 2006).

The gases emission, energy consumption, and biogas produced in black-film anaerobic digestion were estimated based on previous studies, and its detailed introduction was shown in Text S1 (Supple- mentary material). The biogas production in the fermentation stage is 0.35 m³ /kg COD, and the biogas consists of CH4 (55-65%) CO2 (35-45 %), and H2S (0-1 %) (Chen et al., 2018; Balat and Balat, 2009). The emission factors of NO and CO2 produced during biogas combustion were 1.21 g/kg CH4 and 4.47 g/kg CH4, respectively, and the calculated SOX and NO emissions were extremely small and could be ignored (Tsapekos et al., 2019). In addition, approximately 2.7 % of CH4 leaks during anaerobic digestion system operation (Hjort-Gregersen, 2013). According to Song et al. (2021), the electricity consumption of black- film anaerobic digestion is about 0.17 kWh/m³ An electrical effi- ciency of 42 % of the biogas lower heating value (LHV) was considered, and the combined heat and power (CHP) engine electrical efficiency was 39 % (Khoshnevisan et al., 2018; Naroznova et al., 2016). Accordingly, the total energy generated of 12.5KWh/FU was calculated.
20: The wastewater biological treatment used a two-stage anoxic/oxic (A/O) process in R1-R3. In the denitrification stage, the total nitrogen in the influent produced 1 % N2O (Foley et al., 2008). The decomposition of organic matter during the anaerobic reaction in the wastewater pro- duced CH4, and 0.104 kg CH4/COD was used in this study (Sahely et al., 2006).

The gases emission, energy consumption, and biogas produced in black-film anaerobic digestion were estimated based on previous studies, and its detailed introduction was shown in Text S1 (Supple- mentary material). The biogas production in the fermentation stage is 0.35 m³ /kg COD, and the biogas consists of CH4 (55-65%) CO2 (35-45 %), and H2S (0-1 %) (Chen et al., 2018; Balat and Balat, 2009). The emission factors of NO and CO2 produced during biogas combustion were 1.21 g/kg CH4 and 4.47 g/kg CH4, respectively, and the calculated SOX and NO emissions were extremely small and could be ignored (Tsapekos et al., 2019). In addition, approximately 2.7 % of CH4 leaks during anaerobic digestion system operation (Hjort-Gregersen, 2013). According to Song et al. (2021), the electricity consumption of black- film anaerobic digestion is about 0.17 kWh/m³ An electrical effi- ciency of 42 % of the biogas lower heating value (LHV) was considered, and the combined heat and power (CHP) engine electrical efficiency was 39 % (Khoshnevisan et al., 2018; Naroznova et al., 2016). Accordingly, the total energy generated of 12.5KWh/FU was calculated.
21: In this study, a modified groove composting technology was used, which is widely used for manure composting because of its large treat- ment capacity and short fermentation cycle (Luo et al., 2014; Ten Hoeve et al., 2019). In the process of composting, 15 % of the total organic carbon derived from the composting materials was converted to CO2 emissions. The emissions factors of CH4 and N2O were estimated based on the report by IPCC (2006). Microbial bacteria and rice bran were added to improve the composting efficiency. The rice bran was obtained from the internal data of the project and the characteristics were shown in Table S4 (Supplementary material). The data on electricity con- sumption and microbial bacteria consumed were obtained from Dong et al. (2014). All materials added during composting were calculated according to the C/N ratio of 25 in this study.
22: Volatilization, leaching, and runoff of NH3, N2, NO, and NO3 will occur during the land use process, resulting in direct emissions of N2O and NH3 (Wulf et al., 2006). A part of the nitrogen and phosphorus in manure and sludge are also converted to fertilizers (NH4NO3 and TSP), which can be absorbed by plants. The conversion rates are 1.96 g/g N and 2.5 g/g P in manure/sludge, and the uptake rates are 38 % and 29 % (Pan et al., 2019).
23: The environmental impacts of the three PM treatment and disposal routes are obtained by using Recipe Midpoint and Endpoint (Hierar- chical) 1.04. The covered midpoint impact indicators in this study are shown in Table 3. The LCA modeling was conducted by using SimaPro software (version. 9.1, PRé Sustainability).

26: Uncertainty analysis were performed to gain insight into how vari- ations in LCI data (i.e., their 95 % confidence intervals (95 CI) affected final LCA results. We captured statistical uncertainty using the MC analysis (10,000 randomly sampled trials) provided by SimaPro 9.1. Data of uncertainty assessment were I 10 % of LCI data in Table 2. The final uncertainty results were demonstrated in two ways: 1) the error bars of the environmental impact (Fig. 2); and 2) the results of comparing the same environmental impacts for different routes (Fig. S3, Supplementary Material).
27: 3.1. Holistic comparison of environmental impacts from PM treatment and disposal routes

The LCA results of three PM treatment and disposal routes are pre- sented in Table 4. For most impact categories except for TET, FE, GWP, TA, SOD, and PMF, R3 is higher than R1 and R2. Therefore, under the comprehensive consideration of all environmental impacts, R1 and R2 appear to perform better than R3. At present, "synergistic control of environmental pollution and carbon emission" is the most significant strategy in the process of PM treatment and disposal in China, thus the subsequent discussions of this study focus on the major contribution processes and dominant pollutants for the toxicity (HCT, HNCT, FET, and TET) and GWP of three PM treatment and disposal routes (Beyers et al., 2022, Wang et al., 2023, Jiang et al., 2021a, 2021b).
28: <header>3.1.1. Contributions of specific process to different toxicity impacts </header>

The contributions of specific processes to FET (a), TET (b), HCT (c), and HNCT (d) are shown in Fig. 2. For FET, TET, and HCT of R1, the wastewater treatment process is the predominant contributor, ac- counting for %, 99 and %, respectively. This is attributed to the consumption of electricity and the usage of chemicals. While, for HNCT of R1, land use and wastewater treatment are the two main processes, and their contribution percentages are relatively close. Moreover, R2 has the lowest of all toxic effects due to fertilizers substitution during land use, which partially offset the toxic effects. Therein, for FET and TET of R2, the fertilizers substitution in R2 led to environmental benefits of -6.1 kg 1, 4-DB eq and -422 kg 1, 4-DB eq, respectively. For R3, the toxicity impacts of the wastewater treatment process are 1.7-2.1 times

more than R1 and 2.4-2.6 times more than R2, respectively. Electricity consumption and overdosing of PAC, Na2CO3, and NaClO during the wastewater treatment process of R3 constituted the important factors that caused the toxicity impacts. In a word, the indirect release of toxic substances from electricity production and chemical manufacturing plays a predominant role in generating toxic effects, which is consistent with the conclusion of Zhou et al. (2023).
29: <header>3.1.1. Contributions of specific process to different toxicity impacts </header>

The contributions of specific processes to FET (a), TET (b), HCT (c), and HNCT (d) are shown in Fig. 2. For FET, TET, and HCT of R1, the wastewater treatment process is the predominant contributor, ac- counting for %, 99 and %, respectively. This is attributed to the consumption of electricity and the usage of chemicals. While, for HNCT of R1, land use and wastewater treatment are the two main processes, and their contribution percentages are relatively close. Moreover, R2 has the lowest of all toxic effects due to fertilizers substitution during land use, which partially offset the toxic effects. Therein, for FET and TET of R2, the fertilizers substitution in R2 led to environmental benefits of -6.1 kg 1, 4-DB eq and -422 kg 1, 4-DB eq, respectively. For R3, the toxicity impacts of the wastewater treatment process are 1.7-2.1 times

more than R1 and 2.4-2.6 times more than R2, respectively. Electricity consumption and overdosing of PAC, Na2CO3, and NaClO during the wastewater treatment process of R3 constituted the important factors that caused the toxicity impacts. In a word, the indirect release of toxic substances from electricity production and chemical manufacturing plays a predominant role in generating toxic effects, which is consistent with the conclusion of Zhou et al. (2023).
30: <header>3.1.2. Contributions of specific process to GWP </header>

The GHG emissions from electricity consumed and chemicals used in R1-R3 are 101.5 kg CO2/FU, 182.1 kg CO2/FU, and 185.6 kg CO2/FU, which contributed 15.56 9 14.03%, and 20.41 % to their total carbon emissions, respectively (Table S8-S10, Supplementary material). Direct GHG emissions during the composting process in three PM treatment and disposal routes are the main contributors to the GWP, all above 75 % of the total impact (Fig. 2(f)). Moreover, the GWP of the different composting methods differed significantly. The GWP of the mono-composting process in R2 is 1299.9 kg CO2 eq, which is about 1.8 times of the co-composting process (manure and sludge) in R3. This is mainly due to the increased electricity and materials consumption in the operation of the composting system. To achieve the same composting effect as the co-composting (R3), R2 uses more electricity and materials and inevitably produces more GHG. Generally, it is possible to improve environmental benefits by offsetting indirect GHG emissions through the substitution of fertilizers products after the composting process (Sha- koor et al., 2021). However, in this study, these GHG emissions were much smaller than the direct GHG emissions.
31: <header>3.1.3. Contributions of specific process to TA </header>

TA mainly depends on the indirect release of nitrogen or sulfur ox- ides from electricity and material consumption. R1 has the lowest TA (1.4 kg SO2 eq), followed by R3 (2.1 kg SO2 eq) and R2 (4.1 kg SO2 eq). The land use process plays a predominant environmental impact in PM treatment and disposal, accounting for %-98 (Fig. 2(e)). In R2, the generated electricity during the black-film anaerobic digestion process and the fertilizers substitution in the process of land use can have some environmental benefits, but these environmental benefits are still too small. In addition, R3 consumes a large amount of chemicals and elec- tricity, thereby the TA of the wastewater treatment process is higher than R1.
32: <header>3.1.4. Contributions of specific process to FE and ME </header>

Fig. 2(g) and Fig. 2(h) show the two aspects of eutrophication im- pacts on freshwater and marine. Therein, FE mainly depends on the discharge of nitrate and phosphate, and to a lesser extent on ammonia (Prapaspongsa et al., 2010). The land use process is the main contributor to FE, accounting for 75 89 %, and 72 % of the total FE in R1-R3, respectively. This is because the phosphate and nitrate in the manure and sludge leach into the groundwater during the land use process, which in turn causes freshwater eutrophication. Besides, ME is also included to consider the impacts of nitrogen emissions in this study, because one of the goals of treating wastewater is to reduce nitrogen loadings. However, the effluent from the three routes all need to meet the same discharge standard, thereby the ME generated from the three routes are all close and low X 1.3 X and 3.4 X 101 kg N eq, respectively).
33: <header>3.2.1. Contributions of specific pollutants to FET, TET, HCT, and HNCT </header>

To clarify which pollutants in PM are the main hot spots contributing to high FET, TET, HCT, and HNCT, environmental impacts caused by HMs during the land use process of three routes are analyzed, and the results are shown in Fig. 3. Zn is the leading contributor to FET and TET, followed by As, Cr, and Cu. Cd and Pb have less significant effects. It differs slightly from the research results of Stone et al. (2012), who

Table 4 Environmental impacts derived from different PM treatment and disposal routes.

3.2.2. Contributions of specific GHGs to GWP and specific pollutants to TA Recently, the carbon footprints of various industries have gained wide attention due to the concept of "carbon neutrality" (Zhou et al., 2023). As previously mentioned, the direct GHG emissions in the process of composting play a vital role in generating GWP from all PM treatment routes. In this section, we have explored which kind of GHG contributes the most greenhouse effect. The contribution of CO2, CH4, and N2O all accounting for about 50 %, 20 %, and 18 % of the total GWP in R1-R3, respectively (Fig. S1(a) Supplementary material). This is not slightly in line with the study of Liu et al. (2021) in which CO2 and CH4 emis- sions during the PM composting process contributed approximately 36

% and 14 % of the total GWP, which is because they used C-related and N-related emission factors of manure between our and their study may differ. Besides, ammonia emission is the most significant factor in generating TA (Petersen et al., 2007; Anderson et al., 2003). In this study, the contribution of ammonia emission to TA of R1-R3 all >98 (Fig. S1(b), Supplementary material). Fig. 2(f) has indicated that the land use process in R1-R3 is the predominant contributor to TA. It is consistent with the findings of Corbala-Robles et al. (2018), who also showed that TA was mainly caused by NH3 emission during the land use process.

40: <header>3.3. Simulation of a PM treatment and disposal route with nutrients recovery and its environmental impacts </header>

Nowadays, most of the phosphate fertilizer agriculture needs come from exploiting phosphate rock (a limited resource). With the decrease in the total storage of phosphate rock, people are increasingly concerned about the sustainability of global phosphate rock (Sena and Hicks, 2018). Wastewater has become a feasible resource for phosphorus re- covery and recycling. One way to recover phosphorus from wastewater is to precipitate magnesium ammonium phosphate (struvite), which can slow down the phosphorus load in the pipeline and reduce the burden of subsequent treatment (Amann et al., 2018). The current researches on struvite recovery from PM mainly focus on using anaerobic digestion liquid as a raw material (Nagarajan et al., 2023; De Vrieze et al., 2019). Compared with the direct recovery of struvite from PM, the recovery from anaerobic digestate has a higher yield. Anaerobic digestion of PM increases the amount of active phosphorus by 26 %, thus increasing the efficiency of phosphorus removal (De Vrieze et al., 2019; Moody et al., 2009). Therefore, we simulate an optimized process route (R4) for PM
Fig. 3. Contributions of specific pollutants to FET (a), TET (b), HCT(c), and HNCT (d) in R1-R3.

treatment and disposal, that is, the combination of black-film anaerobic digestion-struvite recovery. The system boundary of R4, more detailed information, and the LCI and LCA results are shown in Fig. S2, Table S11-S14, respectively (Supplementary material).

The LCA results show that the HCT, HNCT, GWP, and TA of R4 are 3.5 kg 1, 4-DB eq, 67.9 kg 1, 4-DB eq, 573.7 kg CO2 eq, and 1.2 kg SO2 eq, respectively, which are better than R1-R3. The FET, TET, and FE of R4 perform better than R1 and R3 and worse than R2. Besides, The ME of R4 is also small and close to R1-R3. Fig. 4 presents the main contributors to all environmental impacts of R4. For FET, TET, HCT, and ME, the chemicals consumption in wastewater treatment is the main contrib- utor. While the discharged pollutants, that is, heavy metals (mainly As), GHGs, and N-related gases emissions contribute most to HNCT, GWP, TA, and FE, respectively. These results are similar to the previous analysis of the contribution process and sources of the environmental impacts derived from R1-R3.

Overall, in this study, the combination of struvite recovery with anaerobic digestion will effectively reduce the environmental impact of PM treatment and disposal routes. However, Pedizzi et al. (2018) showed that the amount of struvite recovered from PM was less and the implementation of struvite precipitation did not influence the environ- mental results decisively. We think that the different conclusions be- tween the two studies are due to the study of Pedizzi et al. (2018) did not have a good material flow analysis. In this study, we have accurately calculated the loss of nitrogen and phosphorus after the struvite pre- cipitation process. The reduced nitrogen and phosphorus in wastewater must cause the reduction of chemicals consumption in the process of wastewater treatment. Therefore, we believe that the implementation of struvite precipitation after anaerobic digestion will have an excellent environmental performance on the whole PM treatment system.

Nowadays, most of the phosphate fertilizer agriculture needs come from exploiting phosphate rock (a limited resource). With the decrease in the total storage of phosphate rock, people are increasingly concerned about the sustainability of global phosphate rock (Sena and Hicks, 2018). Wastewater has become a feasible resource for phosphorus re- covery and recycling. One way to recover phosphorus from wastewater is to precipitate magnesium ammonium phosphate (struvite), which can slow down the phosphorus load in the pipeline and reduce the burden of subsequent treatment (Amann et al., 2018). The current researches on struvite recovery from PM mainly focus on using anaerobic digestion liquid as a raw material (Nagarajan et al., 2023; De Vrieze et al., 2019). Compared with the direct recovery of struvite from PM, the recovery from anaerobic digestate has a higher yield. Anaerobic digestion of PM increases the amount of active phosphorus by 26 %, thus increasing the efficiency of phosphorus removal (De Vrieze et al., 2019; Moody et al., 2009). Therefore, we simulate an optimized process route (R4) for PM
Fig. 3. Contributions of specific pollutants to FET (a), TET (b), HCT(c), and HNCT (d) in R1-R3.

treatment and disposal, that is, the combination of black-film anaerobic digestion-struvite recovery. The system boundary of R4, more detailed information, and the LCI and LCA results are shown in Fig. S2, Table S11-S14, respectively (Supplementary material).

The LCA results show that the HCT, HNCT, GWP, and TA of R4 are 3.5 kg 1, 4-DB eq, 67.9 kg 1, 4-DB eq, 573.7 kg CO2 eq, and 1.2 kg SO2 eq, respectively, which are better than R1-R3. The FET, TET, and FE of R4 perform better than R1 and R3 and worse than R2. Besides, The ME of R4 is also small and close to R1-R3. Fig. 4 presents the main contributors to all environmental impacts of R4. For FET, TET, HCT, and ME, the chemicals consumption in wastewater treatment is the main contrib- utor. While the discharged pollutants, that is, heavy metals (mainly As), GHGs, and N-related gases emissions contribute most to HNCT, GWP, TA, and FE, respectively. These results are similar to the previous analysis of the contribution process and sources of the environmental impacts derived from R1-R3.

Overall, in this study, the combination of struvite recovery with anaerobic digestion will effectively reduce the environmental impact of PM treatment and disposal routes. However, Pedizzi et al. (2018) showed that the amount of struvite recovered from PM was less and the implementation of struvite precipitation did not influence the environ- mental results decisively. We think that the different conclusions be- tween the two studies are due to the study of Pedizzi et al. (2018) did not have a good material flow analysis. In this study, we have accurately calculated the loss of nitrogen and phosphorus after the struvite pre- cipitation process. The reduced nitrogen and phosphorus in wastewater must cause the reduction of chemicals consumption in the process of wastewater treatment. Therefore, we believe that the implementation of struvite precipitation after anaerobic digestion will have an excellent environmental performance on the whole PM treatment system.
44: In this section, sensitivity analysis is used to analyze the changes in environmental impacts caused by treating PM with different charac- teristics by applying the same treatment and disposal route (R3). The current results indicate that PM with different characteristics has a certain influence on environmental impacts (Table S6-S7, Supple- mentary material). Therein, it is clear that the environmental impacts generated by a change in PM characteristics decreased with the increase of resource substance (C, N, P content) and the reduction of pollutants concentrations (HMs) in PM. Thus, it is necessary to consider the PM characteristics as an important factor that can enable policymakers to adopt a treatment process suitable for various PM characteristics.
45: A comparison of the environmental impact results of the PM treat- ment and disposal routes is conducted with a confidence level of and the results of the specific environmental impacts are shown in Fig. S3 (Supplementary material). The ME of R2 and R4 showed high uncertainty, which was a result of similar amounts of N-related sub- stance emission. For the comparison of other environmental impacts, the results almost no change. Therefore, the conclusions we obtained before have strong robustness.
46: From the above LCA results, the toxicity impacts derived from the PM treatment and disposal routes mainly depend on the consumed electricity and chemicals in the process of wastewater treatment, thereby exploring feasible methods to reduce energy and chemicals consumption are significantly. Reforming and upgrading the existing wastewater treatment facilities to low energy requirements or intelligent wastewater management systems may be a good solution. For instance,

applying the shortcut nitrogen removal processes such as the nitridation-anammox process to replace the current two-stage anoxic- oxic process (nitrification-denitrification, about 2.38 kWh/ kg N removed) can substantially reduce aeration energy consumption (about 0.90 kWh/ kg N removed) and no carbon requirement (Zhou et al., 2023). Installing an intelligent wastewater management system can monitor the quality of wastewater in real-time (e.g., COD, TN, NH3-N, etc.) and use mathematical models to accurately regulate the amount of aeration and chemicals dosage, which also can realize the reduction of energy and chemicals consumption simultaneously in the wastewater treatment process (Sun et al., 2016; Bello et al., 2016).
47: The direct carbon emissions from the composting process of manure/ sludge play a predominant role in GWP. However, it is still a great challenge to effectively control GHGs emissions in composting process. Previous studies have indicated that the addition of biochar into the PM composting may effectively enhance the reduction of GHGs emissions due to it can partially stabilize the PM (Liu al., 2019; Luo et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2020). Jiang et al. (2021a, 2021b) showed that a wheat straw addition rate of 5 % manure composting can reduce most envi- ronmental impacts, especially GWP and LU, compared with the strategy of manure mono-composting. Furthermore, composting temperature is a key factor affecting microbial nitrogen cycling processes (including nitrification, denitrification, and ammonification) and can significantly affect N2O production. Cui et al. (2019) showed that hyperthermophilic composting (composting temperature > 80 °C) significantly inhibited the activities of ammonia-oxidizing and denitrifying bacteria, and decreased N2O emissions by >90 compared with conventional com- posting. Hence, adding functional assistant materials, or upgrading conventional composting to a hyperthermophilic composting process may be practicable for reducing GHGs emissions during the PM com- posting process.
48: Recovery of resources and energy during PM treatment and disposal can markedly increase environmental benefits and offset the environ- mental burden. Wastewater after the PM dewatering process is treated through anaerobic digestion, which can produce electricity and produce large amounts of orthophosphate. This creates an excellent condition to recovery the nitrogen and phosphorus of PM in the form of struvite. Some studies have shown that struvite is more effective than commer- cially used phosphorus fertilizers, that is, 1 kg produced struvite is enough to fertilize 2.6 ha of arable land (Kumar and Pal, 2015; González-Ponce et al., 2009; Szymanska et al., 2019). Therefore, intro- ducing struvite recovery process is also one of the meaningful optimi- zation strategies for PM treatment.
49: This study quantitatively analyzed the environmental impacts of the typical full-scale PM treatment and disposal routes based on the LCA methodology. The chemicals consumption in treating the wastewater and the electricity consumption in composting the manure/sludge after the dewatering process of PM are the two key contributors to all envi- ronmental impacts of PM treatment and disposal routes. Using green chemicals and controlling the dosage of added chemicals accurately in the wastewater treatment process and selecting co-composting to treat manure/sludge will significantly reduce the environmental burden of PM treatment and disposal routes. The nitrogen and phosphorus re- covery in the form of struvite precipitation from PM has an excellent environmental performance, which is attributed to the environmental benefits derived from fertilizer substitution and the reduced chemicals consumption of the subsequent wastewater treatment process. Besides, PM characteristics have a certain influence on the environmental im- pacts generated from the PM treatment and disposal route. The future

52: This study inevitably also has some limitations. For example, due to the lack of a Chinese background inventory database, the inventory data used for chemicals and materials were modeled globally or for the 'rest of the world' (i.e., other regions than Europe). In addition, life cycle costing analysis has yet to be carried out, so this study did not propose the route selection based on joint consideration of the environmental and economic performances. Overall, although this study has limita- tions, it also provides essential information for developing and imple- menting technologies for treating and disposing of PM.
